clues.c: This city on the Seine is France's capital.
Japan's capital, the world's most populous metro area.
Australia's capital — and no, it's not Sydney.
Egypt's sprawling capital on the Nile.
The capital of Kazakhstan, renamed more than once.
1997 film about a ship that hit an iceberg.
Simba becomes king in this animated Disney film.
Keanu Reeves dodges bullets in this 1999 sci-fi film.
Tarantino film with Vincent, Jules, and a glowing briefcase.
This 1941 Orson Welles film is often called the greatest ever made.
H2O is the chemical formula for this.
The star at the center of our solar system.
The gas humans exhale that plants take in.
The 'powerhouse of the cell'.
This scientist published the theory of general relativity in 1915.
The 'King of Pop'.
British band behind the album 'Abbey Road'.
Singer of 'Rolling in the Deep' and '25'.
Rapper behind 'The Marshall Mathers LP'.
This composer went deaf yet wrote his Ninth Symphony.
The sport played at Wimbledon.
NBA legend who wore #23 for the Bulls.
Country that has won the most FIFA World Cups.
'The Greatest', born Cassius Clay.
This jockey-free race is 'the most exciting two minutes in sports'.
The largest organ of the human body.
The organ that produces insulin.
The number of bones in the adult human body.
The medical term for the collarbone.
The smallest bone in the human body, found in the ear.
The first US president.
He issued the Emancipation Proclamation.
The only president to serve more than two terms.
He resigned in 1974 over the Watergate scandal.
This president was a former actor and 'the Great Communicator'.
The largest planet in our solar system.
The 1969 mission that first landed humans on the Moon.
The red planet.
The galaxy that contains our solar system.
This telescope, launched in 2021, is Hubble's successor.
The largest land animal.
The fastest land animal.
The only mammal capable of true flight.
A group of these big cats is called a pride.
This is the largest animal ever to have lived.
This Italian dish is a flat bread with toppings.
Guacamole is made mainly from this fruit.
This Japanese dish pairs raw fish with vinegared rice.
Champagne can only come from this region of France.
This spice, by weight, is the most expensive in the world.
The longest river in the world by most measures.
The only country that is also a continent.
The largest desert on Earth (it's cold).
The tallest mountain above sea level.
This sea between Israel and Jordan is Earth's lowest land point.
Greek god of the sea.
Norse god of thunder.
Egyptian god of the dead, shown with a jackal head.
In Greek myth, this Titan gave fire to humanity.
The Hindu god of destruction in the Trimurti.
He co-founded Apple with Steve Wozniak.
This company's search engine handles most of the world's queries.
He's credited with inventing the World Wide Web in 1989.
The 'father of the computer', he designed the Analytical Engine.
This 1970s protocol suite lets the internet's networks talk — two acronyms.
'To be or not to be' is spoken by this Danish prince.
These star-crossed lovers are from Verona.
This Scottish play features three witches and 'is this a dagger'.
The Moor of Venice, deceived by Iago.
In this play, a king divides his kingdom among three daughters.
The chemical symbol for gold.
The most abundant element in the universe.
The number of elements in the modern periodic table (as of the 118th).
This scientist created the periodic table in 1869.
This element, symbol Fe, makes up much of Earth's core.
Egyptian queen allied with both Caesar and Mark Antony.
This wall fell in 1989.
The empire founded and ruled by Genghis Khan.
The first emperor of a unified China.
This 1648 set of treaties ended the Thirty Years' War.
The most spoken native language in the world.
'Robot' entered English from a 1920 play in this language.
This language, spoken in the Basque Country, is unrelated to any other.
This constructed language was created by L. L. Zamenhof in 1887.
The writing system of ancient Mesopotamia, pressed into clay.
The only even prime number.
A polygon with ten sides.
This ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
This constant, roughly 2.718, is the base of the natural logarithm.
The number of ways to arrange 5 items in order (5 factorial).
The study of the very cold.
This SI unit measures luminous intensity.
This particle was theorized by Peter Higgs and confirmed in 2012.
The rarest naturally occurring element on Earth.
This 'fifth state of matter' is created near absolute zero.
'I think, therefore I am' was written by this Frenchman.
He wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra' and declared 'God is dead'.
This Greek was Plato's teacher and drank hemlock.
His 'categorical imperative' anchors deontological ethics.
This philosopher's 'veil of ignorance' argues for justice as fairness.

finals.c: This 1969 mission first landed humans on the Moon.
This is the only country that borders both the Atlantic and Indian Oceans and has three capital cities.
This element, atomic number 6, is the basis of all known life.
This Dutch painter cut off part of his own ear and painted 'The Starry Night'.
This four-note opening belongs to Beethoven's symphony of this number.
